informal_statement.text: Given a binary search tree whose in-order traversal is strictly ascending and two keys both present in the tree, return the key of the lowest node that has both keys in its subtree.
preconditions: the input tree is a valid BST
both query keys are present in the tree
required_properties: the returned key appears in the tree
both query keys are in the subtree rooted at the returned key
no proper descendant of the returned key has both query keys in its subtree
edge_cases: both keys equal (returned key equals them)
one key is an ancestor of the other
keys on opposite sides of the root
tree with only a single path

semantic_units.description: BST precondition: the input tree is a valid BST.
common_failure_modes: omitting the BST precondition and solving general-tree LCA
assuming strict ordering within equal keys

semantic_units.description: Presence precondition: both query keys are present in the tree.
common_failure_modes: returning None silently when a key is missing

semantic_units.description: Ancestor property: both query keys lie in the subtree rooted at the returned key.
common_failure_modes: returning any common ancestor instead of the lowest one

semantic_units.description: Lowest: no proper descendant of the returned node contains both keys.
common_failure_modes: using LCA of general trees (parent-pointer style) semantics

semantic_units.description: Key equality case: when p == q, the LCA is that same key.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∃ sub : Tree, IsSubtree sub t ∧ (match sub with | Tree.nil => False | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a) ∧ HasKey sub p ∧ HasKey sub q
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, the returned key is the root of a subtree containing both queries.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∀ (sub : Tree), IsProperSubtree sub t → (match sub with | Tree.nil => True | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a → False) → HasKey sub p → HasKey sub q → False
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, no proper subtree that is not rooted at the returned key contains both queries and sits strictly below the LCA.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → lcaBst t p p = some p
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing the key, the LCA of a key with itself is that key.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ t : Tree, ∀ p q : Int, (∃ x : Int, x = p ∧ x ∈ bstKeys t) ∧ (∃ y : Int, y = q ∧ y ∈ bstKeys t) → True
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The implementation assumes both keys p and q are present in the BST. Model this as a precondition that p and q occur among the keys of the tree.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: let lo : Int := min p q; let hi : Int := max p q; in lo ≤ hi ∧ (lo = p ∨ lo = q) ∧ (hi = p ∨ hi = q)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The code normalizes the inputs by setting lo=min(p,q) and hi=max(p,q), ensuring lo ≤ hi and that lo/hi are exactly the two input keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q : Int), ∃ v : Nat, v = size t ∧ (∀ cur : Tree, cur ≠ Nil → (next cur) has size < size cur) → (loop terminates)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: When the search moves left or right, it strictly decreases the remaining subtree size, so the loop terminates (cannot continue indefinitely).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: let lo := min p q; let hi := max p q; in ∀ cur : Tree, cur ≠ Nil → (p ∈ bstKeys cur ∧ q ∈ bstKeys cur) → (lo ≤ keyOfLCA cur p q ∧ keyOfLCA cur p q ≤ hi)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Maintain that the true LCA key (in the current subtree) lies within the current bounds [lo, hi]. The algorithm only narrows by moving to the side consistent with BST ordering.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ l : Tree, ∀ k : Int, ∀ r : Tree, bst l → bst r → (∀ x ∈ bstKeys l, x < k) → (∀ y ∈ bstKeys r, k < y) → bst (Node l k r)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Assume the tree is a BST: all keys in the left subtree are < node key and all keys in the right subtree are > node key. This justifies the branch conditions hi < k and lo > k.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ cur : Tree, cur = Node left k right → hi < k → (p ∈ bstKeys cur ∧ q ∈ bstKeys cur) → (p ∈ bstKeys left ∧ q ∈ bstKeys left) ∧ lcaKey left p q = lcaKey cur p q
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: If hi < k, then both p and q must lie in the left subtree (by BST ordering), and the LCA in the left subtree is the same as the LCA in the current tree.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ cur : Tree, cur = Node left k right → lo > k → (p ∈ bstKeys cur ∧ q ∈ bstKeys cur) → (p ∈ bstKeys right ∧ q ∈ bstKeys right) ∧ lcaKey right p q = lcaKey cur p q
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: If lo > k, then both p and q must lie in the right subtree, and the LCA in the right subtree equals the LCA in the current tree.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ cur : Tree, cur = Node left k right → lo ≤ k ∧ hi ≥ k → (p ∈ bstKeys cur ∧ q ∈ bstKeys cur) → (k = lcaKey cur p q)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: When the bounds straddle the current node key (lo ≤ k ≤ hi), the algorithm returns k. Under BST ordering and presence of both keys, this node key is exactly the LCA.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ t : Tree, ∀ p q : Int, (p ∈ bstKeys t ∧ q ∈ bstKeys t) → lca_bst t p q ≠ None
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Given the precondition that both keys are present, the loop should never reach Nil; therefore the function cannot return None.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ t : Tree, ∀ p q : Int, bst t → (p ∈ bstKeys t ∧ q ∈ bstKeys t) → ∃! a : Int, lca_bst t p q = Some a ∧ a = lcaKey t p q
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Putting everything together: for a BST where both keys are present, lca_bst returns Some a, and that a is the (unique) lowest common ancestor key of p and q in the tree.
